If you are a Bristol City Council Resident, join Evolve Music’s Southmead Dads Rock group for dads or male caregivers and children under 2!

Unleash your child’s potential with music! Develop emotional control, language, math skills & creativity through play.

STARTS: Saturday 24th January 2026

ENDS: Saturday 28th March 2026

FREQUENCY: Weekly*, Saturday mornings 10am – 11.30am

VENUE: Greenway Centre, Doncaster Road, Southmead, Bristol, BS10 5PY

Discover how child-led musical play helps with developing baby and toddler’s managing big emotions, powerful communication skills, a love of language, a foundation in numbers and their inner rockstar.

Dads Rock (it’s not just a saying, it’s a movement!) is also a chance to build a community with other Dads and share your parenting adventure and musical experiences.

The group is an 8-week adventure and is absolutely free to join. It is made possible through Bristol Family Hubs and you will need to be a Bristol City Council resident to access sessions.

Southmead Dads Rock starts on 24th January and runs until the 28th March (there are no sessions taking place on 14th February or 14th March). Ideally Dads/male caregivers will join the musical revolution for the January start but this is not mandatory, so please do get in contact with us if you can only start after this date.

The Berkshire Youth Bassoon Ensemble (Acer Minor) was formed in September 2010. It got its name as Acer is the genus of maple trees, from which bassoons are made. It is a by invitation ensemble, with a minimum standard of grade 8 for entry. At the moment it is 14 strong, with an age range of 12 to 18. Acer has been lucky enough to perform in many prestigious venues and has won awards at the Music for Youth Festival in Birmingham in the past, as well as prizes at a number of local festivals.

Former members of Acer have gone on to study Music at many top conservatoires and universities both in the UK and abroad. Regardless of what former members of the Acer family go on to do in their careers, they all still play their bassoons and join the current Acer group every summer for an alumni concert in aid of Acer’s charity; The Toby Fund. The Toby Fund has raised thousands of pounds over the years, in memory of one of the founding members, with the money going to maintain instruments, purchase accessories and fund masterclasses and consultation lessons for bassoonists and contrabassoonists in Berkshire. The Toby Fund and Acer will be hosting our first national event in March 2023, inviting players and teachers from all over the UK to join us for masterclasses, talks and ensembles.

13. About Opportunity:
Music Therapy is not the same as music tuition: its purpose is not to teach someone how to play an instrument but to allow them to translate their emotions and psychological processes into expressive music in order to release feelings, repair them and heal. Music can offer an alternative method of communication or expression where words are not an option or are inadequate. Some feelings are just too heavy for words to carry – but music always finds a way to express them. The aim is to bring out the client’s creativity in order to develop positive changes in behaviour, emotional and physical well-being and communication.

Music is a powerful emotional medium which can affect people profoundly And seems to give us permission to connect with our deepest emotions. These emotions can sometimes be melancholy and sad, sometimes angry, sometimes exuberant, joyous and life affirming.

Moreover, this intense emotional response to music seems to be unimpaired by illness or disability.

13. About Opportunity:
Triggshire String Orchestra is based in Camelford with young people from North Cornwall: from Bude to Wadebridge and Bodmin. Every Wednesday a bunch of very enthusiastic string players aged 8 to 16 and some parents come together to have fun playing music and preparing for performances. Under the direction of Sarah Routly and Kate Tidball  the members experience all the benefits of the social and musical aspects of ensemble playing. It boosts confidence and gives a sense of purpose for the weekly instrumental lessons.

The rehearsals are from 5-6 on Wednesdays during term time. In the past, activities included a trip to Birmingham Symphony Hall as part of Music for Youth festival. Once a term we get together with other string ensembles from Cornwall as the SuperStrings group, rehearse together and play for friends and family. This is quite a spectacle with well over 50 young players!


40 YEARS OF TRIGG
Sarah Routly - Musical Director

I have played the violin and recorders for 50 years, I also play Cello, Viola and to a much lesser extent Tuba, Euphonium, Guitar, piano and flute! An eclectic mix! I am always learning, I don’t believe instrumentalists ever stop learning!

Strings wise, I teach Violin, Cello and Viola which I absolutely love. There’s nothing better than seeing others progress and flourish, the sense of satisfaction and pride they feel, as do I! 

Experience wise, I’ve played in many orchestras, in the North East and in the Southwest. I grew up in Middlesbrough where I played in the Teeside Youth orchestra, before switching to the Cornwall Youth Orchestra in mid 1980s when I was being taught by Karen Frost and latterly by William Lovell

I’ve played Violin and Cello in quartets for weddings and events, also playing violin in a Scottish dance band for weddings which was huge fun, especially playing on a Tall Ship sailing out of Charlestown Harbour!

I currently lead the Launceston Orchestra and a leader in other orchestras, currently leader of the South West Philharmonia & Chorus which is a great honour

I teach Recorder also. Recorder has always been a very important aspect of my musical career and I have a real passion for Baroque and Renaissance music. I’ve performed the Sammartini Recorder concerto with Bude orchestra in recent years, and this was a highlight of my recorder journey so far! 

I’m excited to take Triggshire Strings even further on their already accomplished journey. If you’re not at grade 1 yet, don’t worry. I can happily write parts to suit individual ability but to play in ensembles makes a huge impact on the learner, both musically and as we often see, in their social development and confidence in general. 



Joining the Orchestra

New players are always welcome. The minimum level to join is from around grade 1 onwards for any bowed instrument. As it can be a scary idea to come to a new group of people, we offer taster sessions. Just come and give it a go, nobody needs to play all the “right” notes, we love them all. ??

Please contact Sarah Routly on sroutly@cmst.co.uk to find out more.

13. About Opportunity:
Triggshire Wind Orchestra was formed in 1984 by the current musical director, Janet Elston, to provide young musicians from North Cornwall the experience of playing in a large ensemble.

The ‘Shire of Trigg’ was an old administrative area dating back to the early medieval period and stretches down the north coast from Bude to Padstow while incorporating the inland moorland villages.

Triggshire Wind Orchestra has performed on the Scilly Isles, in Bristol and London, where they played at Southwark Cathedral, Hyde Park and performed for schools in Lewisham.

They have worked with and performed alongside ‘Live Brass’, a quintet led by Kate Moore, principal trumpet of the BBC Concert Orchestra and have been on tour to Belgium, where they played at the Menin Gate Ceremony. In 2017 Trigg joined forces with Budehaven voices and Bodmin College Jazz Orchestra for a tour of Lake Garda in Italy. More recently, rehearsals continued online throughout the pandemic, and everyone is now enjoying being able to make music together once again.


Janet Elston - Musical Director

Janet grew up in Rotherham, Yorkshire, where she learned to play the clarinet and saxophone. Having played in several bands and orchestras, she decided that a music career was for her after realising her love for music. Janet played clarinet in the National Youth Wind Orchestra for several years, when her passion for wind orchestras was born. After taking her degree at Huddersfield, where she won the Forbes Wind Prize, she then did a post-grad at Bretton Hall. Throughout her years of playing, she has performed in several ensembles and orchestras, including a professional clarinet quartet called the Milton Ensemble, which toured the country. 

Janet was delighted to get her first teaching post in Cornwall, where she has remained many years later. She still plays professionally and is Ensemble Manager for the Cornwall Music Service Trust, conductor of the Triggshire Wind Orchestra and has recently retired from conducting the Cornwall Youth Wind Orchestra after 29 years. Any spare time that she has is spent riding her horse!
“Janet was musical director of the Cornwall Youth Wind Orchestra when I was a young musician in Cornwall in the 1990s. Her incredible patience, enthusiasm and inspiration ensured that I left Cornwall as a young adult with a real love of music. I have continued to enjoy playing throughout my adult life with so many happy memories of Janet, and the CYWO.”
— Catherine
The Influence of North Cornwall in Music

North Cornwall has a unique musical heritage characterised by traditional folk and brass band music. This heritage has played a significant role in shaping the Orchestra's repertoire and its approach to music-making.

The Triggshire Wind Orchestra regularly performs traditional music of Cornwall, such as sea shanties and folk songs. This music is integral to North Cornwall's cultural identity and heritage.

The traditional music of Cornwall has had a significant impact on the Triggshire Wind Orchestra's repertoire, which is characterised by a blend of traditional and contemporary music.
LISTEN TO TRIGG

Joining the Orchestra

The Triggshire Wind Orchestra is open to amateur musicians of all ages and abilities. Members are invited to audition for the Orchestra and are expected to commit to regular rehearsals and performances.

The Orchestra plays a vital role in music education in the community, providing opportunities for young musicians to learn and perform alongside experienced musicians.

The Triggshire Wind Orchestra actively participates in community outreach programs and performs regularly at local events and venues. The Orchestra is committed to promoting music education and appreciation in the community.

13. About Opportunity:
Are you passionate about music? Do you play an instrument? Whether you're a seasoned musician or just starting your musical journey, we have an exciting opportunity for you. Welcome to our multi-instrument ensemble, where we welcome all instruments. On this page, we'll delve into the vibrant world of our ensemble, its welcoming atmosphere, diverse repertoire, and the joyous experiences it offers. Join us as we explore the magic of music!

Embracing All Instruments: A Welcoming Community

At our ensemble, we believe that music knows no boundaries. We welcome musicians of all levels and any instrument, from wind and brass to violin, the versatile keyboards, and the rhythmic beats of percussion.

Joining our ensemble is simple. All you need is the ability to make a sound on your instrument. We have a dedicated team of staff members and volunteers who are eager to support you on your musical journey. Whether you're a beginner or a seasoned player, we are here to help you grow and develop your musical talents.

Custom Music for All Abilities

One of the unique aspects of our ensemble is that our music and arrangements are tailored to our band members. We understand that each musician has abilities and limitations, and we want everyone to feel comfortable and confident while playing. Our band leaders arrange the repertoire that allows you to showcase your skills and play what you can manage.

Whether we're exploring the classical masterpieces of Mozart, delving into the captivating world of jazz, rocking to popular tunes, or immersing ourselves in the enchanting melodies of show tunes, our repertoire covers a wide range of musical genres. You'll be able to explore different styles and expand your musical horizons.
JOIN THE GROUP


The Joy of Rehearsals: Fun and Friendship

When you join our ensemble, you become part of a vibrant community united by a love for music. Every week, we gather for rehearsals filled with excitement, camaraderie, and, most importantly, fun! Our warm-up games get us energised and ready to embark on a musical adventure.

During rehearsals, we encourage improvisation, allowing you to express yourself and unleash your creativity. It's a time of exploration and personal growth as you discover new musical possibilities. The joyous experience of playing in this group setting is truly unparalleled and a stepping stone to the many other fantastic ensembles Cornwall has to offer.

Join Us at St. Michaels School in Helston

Our rehearsals take place on a Saturday morning at St. Michaels School in Helston. Located in a serene environment, the school is ideal for our musical gatherings, and we have access to school keyboards, drum kit and percussion. We meet fortnightly, allowing you to balance your passion for music with your other commitments.

The rehearsals last an hour and a half, starting at 10:30, which allows parents plenty of time to explore the local amenities. We also have a drink and biscuit break to recharge and socialise with fellow musicians. It's a time to connect, share experiences, and forge lasting friendships with people who share your love for music.
Varenna Watkiss - Musical Director

Verena, a proud Cornish musician, honed her flute and piano skills from a young age. She studied music at Helston Community College, joining their jazz band and taking up the saxophone. Pursuing her passion, she earned a jazz and popular music degree at Middlesex University, followed by a PGCE in Music Education. For a decade, she served as head of music at a local secondary school. Verena's talent led her to the Cornwall and National Youth Jazz orchestras. Now, she leads the thriving Cornish Roots big band while regularly performing in local bands and shows.

Not only a dedicated bandleader, but Verena also remains an active player, relishing the opportunity to perform regularly in local bands and captivating shows. Currently residing in the vibrant Helston area, she passionately imparts her expertise as she teaches woodwind, keyboards and contributes to the curriculum at nearby schools. Verena's vibrant presence in the local music scene has undoubtedly left an indelible mark, inspiring aspiring musicians and bringing the joy of music to all who encounter her.

13. About Opportunity:
The choir meets every Thursday evening during term time from 6.30 pm till 8.00 pm in the Old Cathedral School. They perform a variety of styles of music, ranging from classical pieces such as Handel's Zadok the Priest, Mozart's Ave Verum and Haydn's The Heavens Are Telling, to modern songs like Eric Whitacre's The Seal Lullaby, John Rutter's Look At The World and Russell Pascoe's Celtic Blessing. They also perform lighter pieces such as You Raise Me, Goodnight Sweetheart, And So It Goes, and folk songs such as Londonderry Air. 

The CYCC has performed at various venues, including The Mince Theatre, Truro Cathedral, Carnglaze Caverns, The Eden Project, Tate St Ives and different churches where audiences have praised their high-quality performances and their ability to captivate listeners. 

In addition to their concerts, the choir participates in various educational and community activities, such as workshops, masterclasses and outreach programmes. 

The choir always seeks new members and welcomes singers of all ages and abilities. They are committed to providing a safe, supportive and enjoyable environment for everyone to grow and develop their musical skills while having fun. 

So, if you want to join a choir and make new friends, the Cornwall Youth Chamber Choir could be a perfect choice.

REHEARSALS

Rehearsals for the CYCC Youth Chamber Choir take place at The Old Cathedral School (OCS) from 6.30 pm to 8 pm. Parents should drop off and pick up their children from the entrance across the grass at the side of the Cathedral. Occasionally, the rehearsal venue may need to be changed; you will be notified of any changes via text.

We ask everyone to support the CYCC Facebook page and Twitter, as it is essential to keep a high profile and keep up to date with rehearsals and concerts.

If you are not at a rehearsal where times and dates are discussed, information will be emailed (and texted) to you, and you will be notified of any changes in time, date or rehearsal venue. We need everyone there for the parts to balance, so please make every effort to attend once you have committed to a concert and put the dates in your diary in advance.

We encourage friends who may be interested in singing with us to come along to an open rehearsal, and we love having friends and family at our concerts. 

Please note that the dress code for concerts, unless otherwise stated, is ALL BLACK: girls should wear a mid/long-sleeved top, below-knee skirts and tights (or trousers) and shoes; men/boys should wear long-sleeved button-up shirts, long trousers and black shoes.

13. About Opportunity:
The Cornwall Junior Choir was established in January 2001 as part of an initiative to promote singing in primary schools in Cornwall. The National Foundation for Youth Music awarded the choir a grant under their Singing Challenge programme. The partnership between Truro Cathedral and Cornwall Council Music Service Department founded the choir, which comprises young singers from all over Cornwall.

The choir has performed in various venues, including traditional places like Truro Cathedral and unconventional locations such as Tate St Ives, Gwennap Pit, and The National Maritime Museum. They have also performed in other cathedrals in Gloucester, Exeter, and Scilly. Along with their regular commitments, the choir has shared what they have learned with their audiences and improved their skills and confidence. They have had the opportunity to perform with Aled Jones and participate in a "Children in Need" concert.

In September 2015, the choir's support structure changed slightly but maintained close ties with the Cathedral. The Cornwall Music Service Trust now oversees the choir, providing them with a secure future. The choir is excited about the possibilities for their future under this new arrangement.

Jo Saville - Musical Director

After a musical youth of Cornish brass bands and swing bands (flugel horn/trumpet/saxophone), along with singing in choirs, gigging with a soul band and gaining grade 8 piano, Jo graduated from Exeter University with a BA in music, where she performed for the Queen as part of the University Saxophone Quartet and was the Musical Director of the Jazz Orchestra. Jo also developed her love of singing further here, by performing in a gospel choir, jazz quintet and stage productions.

Following her PCGE from The Institute of Education (University of London), Jo taught music in many schools, in both classroom & peripatetic capacity,  and greatly enjoyed the varied experiences that this created. Years later, Jo has been at the helm of Rock Choir in Cornwall, creating a safe & fun space where members get to sing out some feel-good favourites each week and escape the stresses of daily life, whilst building friendships and experiencing some amazing performance opportunities along the way.  

Jo strongly believes that music is a powerful, positive force and loves that singing is accessible to everyone. She is really excited about working with the Cornwall Junior Choir for the foreseeable future. 

REHEARSALS

As previously, rehearsals run from 10-11.30 am (unless otherwise indicated by email or text)

Drop off and pick up are outside the Old Cathedral School, across the grass at the side of Truro Cathedral. (if you are not sure where that is, please ask).

We all must meet in one place! You will be notified of any changes. Please remember that no parking facilities are available at the Cathedral for Junior Choir parents.

As always, please encourage anyone who might like to be part of the choir to come along to a rehearsal to see what it is like; the first two Saturdays of the term will be held as ‘open rehearsals’, so the more, the merrier.

Subs per child per term are £40 and £70 for two siblings in the choir, and this is handled through the Cornwall Music Service Trust Website. 

Choir uniform - All Black unless otherwise stated (including black socks/tights and shoes)
COME ALONG

You're welcome to come along any Saturday in term-time from 10.00 - 11.30 at Truro Cathedral. We’ll meet at the entrance to The Old Cathedral School,  across the grass at the side of the Cathedral.

Choir secretary Julia Carter and musical director Jo Saville will be there to welcome you and answer any questions about the choir's organisation and activities.

The small mixed choir always welcomes new members. Anyone aged from 6 to 13 years can join. Young singers come from all over Cornwall weekly for expert tuition under the musical director.

 If you have any concerns or questions, please get in touch with Julia Carter (details below).

For more information about the Junior and Youth Choirs please contact Julia Carter: 07812 827 033 or 

01872 573 455 (eves) or email: julia@ccjc.org.uk

13. About Opportunity:
Prepare to be enchanted by CMST’s breathtaking musical supergroup the StringStrings Ensemble! This illustrious group harmoniously combines musicians with a range of abilities; specialising in violin, viola, cello, and double bass. This ensemble dynamically evolves with every term by amalgamating smaller ensembles from all around the county, culminating in a grand orchestral masterpiece. Their captivating concerts, frequently enhanced by percussion, woodwind, and brass accompaniment, are the highlight of each term. Under the mentorship of our dedicated teachers and with a substantial group size, they fearlessly tackle complex compositions, showcasing an unparalleled blend of talent, dedication, and sheer passion on the stage! 

At SuperString performances, the stage comes alive with a dynamic fusion of strings, brass, woodwind, and percussion talent! This collaboration gives students an incredible chance to perform within a larger-than-life orchestra, exploring a repertoire and vibrant sound that could otherwise be beyond their wildest musical dreams. Get ready to be part of this electrifying musical synergy!
Musical Director - Karen Frost

Having taught the violin and viola for over 30 years, Karen enjoys teaching a mix of ages and abilities and believes that every child, whatever their background should be given the opportunity to learn a musical instrument and experience the joy of playing in an ensemble for its life enhancing qualities.

Along with Superstrings she also directs the St. Austell Chamber Orchestra for pupils in the St. Austell/Roseland area, giving students an opportunity to play with others and enjoy a range of musical styles from pop to baroque!

Music making is an integral part of everyday life in Cornwall and in Karen’s spare time and she is actively involved in music making for many local orchestras and choral societies, notably Three Spires Singers, Truro Choral Society, Cornish Sinfonia and many more as well as performing with a professional string quartet which plays regularly for weddings and other events.

13. About Opportunity:
Carrick Strings was formed in October 2022 when the equivalent much loved ensemble - Fal River Strings - could no longer operate and was sorely missed.  Many of the Fal River Strings members managed to successfully integrate into other existing strings groups in Cornwall, but a few members were left stranded!  Hence the drive to create the Carrick Strings ensemble.

Ensemble playing is an important and motivating part of learning an instrument - to develop playing and listening techniques, working as a team with other players, opening up more opportunities and for the social aspects too. 

Carrick Strings tries to choose a varied repertoire from Classical, Film, Pop, Jazz to Cornish.  We invite young players of all ages and abilities and adapt the music according to ability.

Rehearsals take place at:

Falmouth School

Thursday’s, (term time only)

4:30pm - 5:30pm

Superstrings:

Carrick Strings is also part of the much bigger SuperStrings; the SuperStrings provide a vital role in music education in the community, providing opportunities for young musicians to learn and perform alongside more experienced musicians - it is a profoundly magical and exhilarating group to be a part of.

Lucetta Radcliffe - Musical Director of Carrick Strings in Falmouth

Lucetta Radcliffe is a teacher, musician and composer. She studied music at Goldsmiths College in London with a particular focus on composition. After her studies she moved to Cornwall and worked for National Foundation for Youth Music as a community musician for CYMAZ. She also collaborated with a variety of arts practitioners as part of Luminos and worked with young people to create participant-led and devised performances. After this she enjoyed teaching piano for many years and has worked as an Associate Lecturer in Music Theatre at Amata, Falmouth University. She performs (vocals, flute and accordion)  regularly with Radio Rio, a four piece Brazilian/Latin band and is the music director and conductor of River Notes, a community choir based in Falmouth. Most recently she has written a new musical called Bal Maiden and is currently producing it with Cobb Riddle and Spall Music Theatre company. This has given her the opportunity to return to her first musical love of composition. 

Fathyma Parker - Strings Teacher and Musician

Fathyma Parker is musician, violin teacher and eager orchestral player whose main occupation is looking after dogs! She is the mother of two young ambitious musicians. Music is her passion, hobby and joy. She is a member of several orchestras and has participated in a variety of ensembles including; String orchestra, chamber music and contemporary music ensembles.
Joining the Orchestra

17. About Organisation:
By enabling students of all generations, backgrounds and communities to enjoy a LIFE including music making and participation, we seek to grow and support the HEALTH of a musical COMMUNITY for now and for the FUTURE.

Music for LIFE

We believe that music is not merely a hobby but a way of living which can positively impact everyone's emotional and physical wellbeing. At CMST, we aim to create an inclusive atmosphere of music making that all can enjoy, characterised by the care and compassion to enrich all of our students’ lives into the future.

Music for the FUTURE

We aim to inspire our students, encouraging imagination and creativity, and welcoming new developments in technology and innovation to allow ever wider access to Musical Education. We encourage environmental awareness and sustainability in order to secure the future of new and existing musical communities.

Music for the COMMUNITY

We recognise community is at the heart of music making. We respect people, value diversity, and are committed to encouraging inclusivity throughout the music community within Cornwall and beyond. We value and foster partnerships within the local community and businesses in order to create and sustain new musical opportunities for all, and to nurture happy and healthy musical communities.

Music for HEALTH

We believe that music can be a key to the better understanding of our own emotional responses, and to improving mental health - regardless of age, ability or ways of communicating. We are committed to promoting this understanding throughout Cornwall and beyond. Our ambition is to provide access to Music Therapy for all of those in need of its support, to overcome difficulties, and build resilience to brighten their futures.

1. Opportunity Name: Constructor - Moor to Sea Music Collective
13. About Opportunity:
Constructor = A creator or builder of music using multiple methods of composition, direction and conducting.
 
The Constructor Project has developed in response to Moor to Sea’s work with young people and young adults attending Lifeworks community projects over the last 3 years. We noticed emerging themes in interests and skills, as we respond to the young people’s communicated preferences.
We found a group of musical, creative, talented young people and young adults, who need to express themselves through music. Sometimes by playing instruments but also by using things like movement, gestures, and art to direct other musicians. With Youth Music Funding, we were able to develop a dedicated project to delve into this further – What is an Orchestra? Is that accessible to us? What is a conductor or composer? Is that accessible to us?
If not, then what are the alternatives?!
This year-long exploration into “What are the alternatives?” has involved links with professional orchestras, visits from orchestral and non-orchestra musicians, experiments on traditional and non-traditional instruments, inventing and testing out varied methods of composing, making musical decisions, directing, structuring music, combining sounds – and finding ways to communicate all of this without relying on words.
After our Easter intensive week, we have 3 distinct pieces emerging, which we are looking to perform, or have performed by another ensemble in the very near future. Keep an eye on our social media for updates on when you can hear the debut performances of exciting original, new works.

1. Opportunity Name: UnGroup - Moor to Sea Music Collective
13. About Opportunity:
Are you a young person who likes the idea of being in a band, but not sure where to start?
 
Want to make music with others, but not face to face?
Are you a fabulous musician keen to collaborate and experiment?
A vocalist or songwriter looking to work with others?
An aspiring producer or beatmaker wanting to influence?
 
Come & build a musical UnGroup with us.
We’d love to see you all
UnGroup will be available to all young people, connecting us supportively to a musical community. You will influence the music choices and hopefully, some of you will be originating and creating resources for us.
BandLab will be used as a platform for UnGroup members to practice individual parts to a specific song or composition. You will get the opportunity to be creative with your instrument, practice and then record your part.
For those that would prefer, there can be complete creative licence to originate your own arrangement. For those of you that would like more support, we’ll provide accessible guides in how to create a part for yourself. We’ll produce videos to demonstrate chord progressions and timings; guides for what notes are in chords; how you might position your fingers on keyboard; what notes work for transposing instruments; examples of rhythmic patterns; harmony guides for vocalists etc.
We will give you what you need in order to join us at the level you are. We are also working to ensure that people without computers or instruments at home can join UnGroup.
We’ll make a base layer for each musical framework so everyone will work in sync. Then, when you submit your recordings back, we can mix it with others’ parts to create a fuller band. BandLab has a great Digital workspace where you can create MIDI recordings and mix beats, so if this is your thing you will be busy!
Moor to Sea believes in co-creating music together. With our approach, everyone is responsible for creating, developing and owning the musical space. We will be building this project together.

1. Opportunity Name: Exeter Young Strings: Mini Music Makers
13. About Opportunity:
In Mini Music Makers children take part in a fortnightly group lesson which comprises movement games and musical stories which have a very positive and dynamic effect on children’s music-making and their understanding of music in general. By exploring music through movement children gain a physical experience of what the music feels like. They work on their own, in pairs and with others in the class to explore basic musical concepts such as pulse, notation, bar time and phrasing but in a creative and fun environment.   

The children also learn a variety of skills that will be helpful to them when they learn to play an instrument.  Things such as learning about how to use the body effectively, being inventive and creative, learning to respond by listening and by watching, being able to work on their own as well as part of a group, thinking quickly, being well-coordinated – all critical skills to being a good musician. 

Towards the end of the course violins and cellos are introduced. As the children will already be familiar with pitch and rhythm moving onto the instruments just becomes a natural progression. Even if the children don’t choose to play these instruments the skills they will have learnt we be of great benefit for any instrument they choose.

Legacy A Capella (LEGS)
 Membership of LEGS is  drawn from members of  the Legacy Choir by invitation/audition. 17. About Organisation:
The North Devon Music Centre is a non profit organisation that helps young (and young at heart) people who are learning to play an instrument to enjoy making music together through ensemble playing.
 
Learning to play together not only requires new skills to those learnt as a solo player, but also provides the opportunity to make new friends, experience music in a whole new way and helps in becoming a better all-round musician.
 
We meet weekly through school term time and hold regular concerts where we showcase to family, friends and other music lovers. We are made up of 8 different groups of players, led by experienced musicians, catering for all levels, all ages and all types of instruments!
 
Please have a look around our site to find out more, or contact us today.

17. About Organisation:
Welcome to the RootNotes website!
​
RootNotes is a Bideford-based community music organisation, run by local musician Becki Driscoll.
​
RootNotes offers music workshops for all ages and all abilities in a variety of different settings. Whether you are a confident player or just starting out on your musical journey RootNotes offers opportunities to join in and and take part.
​
Our weekly music groups encourage people to have fun whilst learning music and song, with an emphasis on 'hands-on' learning. We can provide instruments for sessions, or help you to develop skills on your own instrument within the group.
Becki Driscoll Community Musician
Young performers with RootNotes
RootNotes music workshops are suitable for schools, preschools, SEN settings, care homes and community events. Workshops can be tailored to suit a theme or a specific group of people.
​
If you are interested in coming along to one of the music groups or would like to book a RootNotes music workshop for your setting please get in touch!
​​

13. About Opportunity:
Launching in Autumn 2022, a group of players from the band offered their time and assistance to any efforts toward growing more brass playing in the local area, and integrate those players into the Training Band, and then Senior Band when ready. The Academy was formed, which quickly split into an Academy Band, and tuition sessions for our ‘new starters’.

We have met lots of local young people since, seeking to start a brass instrument or join their first band. We have often been able to loan a brass instrument to learn with, and music provided as well. We are lucky to have Adrian Harvey and Matt Sherwood to run the ‘new starter’ tuition sessions. The band and new starters are challenged weekly, including learning about how to conduct themselves ready for performances and how ensemble rehearsals work, while learning new pieces and improving them week on week.

We love to see the enjoyment from the players and their families in rehearsal, performing and when having a joke together, because don’t forget, playing in an ensemble is fun!

The success of our young members and the running of the Academy is with thanks to the Friends of the Band and all of STB's supporters, which we value and appreciate.

As many of the members are loaning our instruments, we also offer support with taking care of them. Josie Sherwood, running the Academy Band, enjoys holding Brass maintenance sessions to teach them how to take care of and clean their instruments.

We have a strong and growing membership in the academy band, particularly from our new starter players graduating into the Academy Band. To help with this transition, we have supported some of our players with our STB Tuition Grant. This contributes to the tuition costs so they can continue their learning while attending the Academy Band. We recognise that finding tuition locally is a challenge, and for some a financial barrier that we want to help reduce, with the goal of creating more brass musicians in the area.

We have no doubt that soon enough some of our Academy players will be graduating into the Training Band.

We like to hold an Open evening in September to tell you all about how the Academy works, but we would love to hear from any young people in the area looking to take up a brass instrument or join a band at any time. We meet at St Francis Hall from 7-7:45pm every Friday.

Why not ask us about joining or find out more about the STB Grant by emailing us at info@sidmouthtownband.co.uk
17. About Organisation:
There has been a band in Sidmouth for over 150 years, with the earliest record being a picture of the band taken in 1862.

By the turn of the century, the Band had become part of the local Army Volunteers, and had mixed brass and woodwind instruments. By 1908, the band was changing back to all brass instruments and in 1911 the transformation was complete.

The band was forced to put their kit away in 1914, but reformed in 1919, winning prizes in 1929, 1930 and 1931 at Crystal Palace. History repeated itself in 1939, when the instruments were laid aside once again and many of the band went off to war. In 1947, the band was reformed, becoming affiliated to the SWBBA.
20. Extra Notes: Josie Sherwood at  info@sidmouthtownband.co.uk

13. About Opportunity:
Founded in 2016, South Devon Youth Jazz aims to provide a pathway for the emerging musician into the world of jazz and improvisation.


As a result of a collaboration between jazz musicians, educators and funding partners, its aim is to provide the young musicians of South Devon with an introduction to playing jazz together in a fun and encouraging environment.


Based in Totnes, the project is based around approximately fortnightly workshops with local jazz tutors Sam Massey and Jade Gall and termly masterclasses with top jazz educators.

There is no strict minimum standard to attend our sessions, but we would estimate that students need to be able to read music to a reasonable extent and be approximately a Grade 2 technical standard on their instrument (but no need to have taken exams).


In addition, we work with funding partners Devon & Torbay Music Education Hubs and the Devon Youth Jazz Orchestra to assist with the county’s Jazz Development Programme, delivering outreach workshops in schools and delivering BIG Jazz Days in partnership with regional and national jazz partners.

Exeter Community Family Orchestra provides a common meeting ground, regardless of music taste, experience or ability. It promotes shared experiences across generations and provides a musical family for those living on their own.

The big Friendly Street-Band is performing live happy street music from all over the world, by heart and with some movements.
Composed by wind instruments, drums, and any other acoustic portable instrument. We do some theatre and choreography experiments as well as music and improvisation during the rehearsals.

13. About Opportunity:
The Reed Ensemble uses all sizes of clarinets from soprano to contra-bass, and all sizes of saxophones. The larger instruments are provided by the Music Centre (the bass clarinet is called ‘Winston’ and the contra-bass is called ‘Connie’). We also have a baritone saxophone.
The repertoire is a wide range of classical, jazz and light music, specially arranged for these instruments by Marina and others.
The Ensemble plays in Music Centre concerts and often gives performances at other concerts and events.
Instruments:
clarinets of all sizes: E♭, B♭, alto, bass, contra-bass; saxophones.
Grades:
3 or over. Previous experience with the larger instruments is not necessary.
Directed by:
Marina Kummer. Marina has taught clarinet, flute and other wind instruments in Devon for many years. She is a graduate of Dartington College of Arts, where she studied with David Rix and Roger Fallows. She is music librarian at the Dartington International Summer School.
17. About Organisation:
Torbay and South Devon Music Centre provides the opportunity for musicians to enjoy playing instruments and improve musical skills by performing together. Orchestras and bands are led by experienced musicians and there are opportunities to try new and endangered instruments and to play in concerts as well as weekly rehearsals. Membership is £45 per term per student. Bursaries and reductions are available for Fast Forward groups and families.

"Music Centre is for young musicians living in Torbay, Teignbridge or the South Hams. You can be any age and it doesn’t matter what sort of school you go to. You may be learning to play your instrument at school, or at home or at your teacher’s home – or you may not be having lessons at all. We also welcome parents and other not-so-young musicians. You need to be able to play a suitable instrument – have a look at the list on this page. Players of all standards can enjoy Music Centre, from beginners who can play a few tunes to experts who have been playing for some time. If you have passed any music exams (grades 1 to 8) that will help you to choose a suitable group. If not, don’t worry – we will advise you. It helps if you can read music for your instrument but don’t worry if you can’t – you will get plenty of help. You do not need any previous experience of playing in an ensemble or orchestra.

"Music Centre is for young musicians living in Torbay, Teignbridge or the South Hams. You can be any age and it doesn’t matter what sort of school you go to. You may be learning to play your instrument at school, or at home or at your teacher’s home – or you may not be having lessons at all. We also welcome parents and other not-so-young musicians. 13. About Opportunity:
Lullabubbas is We Need Music's Early Years music making project. We co-create child-centred music making opportunities for babies and young children with their caregivers, both in community and childcare settings. We place high value upon child-led musical play and exploration, creating spaces where very young children can take the lead and shape their own experiences. We recognise the importance of caregiver interactions and the valuable opportunities for increasing bonds that can develop. We foster supportive settings where relationships between families and caregivers can be built, acknowledging the value of raising children in community, reducing isolation and increasing social connections. Lullabubbas offers a place to create, to connect and to express, which impacts upon wellbeing for young children and their caregivers. We aspire to equality of access for all within our sessions and strive to reduce the barriers which can limit participation. We create spaces full of child led-exploration and wonder, opportunities to be immersed in beautiful soundscapes and exciting multi-sensory experiences.

Lullabubbas runs sessions in Torbay and South Hams, Devon. ​
We work across many settings including: 
with parents/carers and their children in community settings
with babies and young children in nursery and pre-school settings
at special events, festivals and forest schools
with Early Years practitioners who would like to feel more confident using music with the children they support.
Join a Lullabubbas session
We have lots of new sessions launching soon.
Please book upcoming sessions here.

13. About Opportunity:
Are you in a band, at school, college or home educated, live across BCP, Southampton, IOW or Portsmouth, and fancy getting some fantastic auditioning and gigging experience?

STEP 1: Complete the application form below 

Please note that confirmation emails aren’t generated automatically from the form but as long as you get to the Thank You message at the end, consider it submitted successfully.

STEP 2: Video audition: 

Your video should be of your band performing 1 track of around 3 minutes which showcases your collective talent and enthusiasm.
At this first stage it doesn’t need to be polished or professional – we just want to see/hear an example of a song you are rehearsing or have performed, so, a good video you’ve taken on your phone is fine!
TIP – try to adjust your dynamic/volume levels for the purposes of the video audition so that we can hear all the parts in the video submitted. In the past we’ve received videos where we can only hear one part, or no vocals at all for example, because everyone is playing too loud for the small space they’re in and the device they’re recording on… don’t worry – in the real thing you’ll be able to give us the full throttle energy and the dynamic best suited to your style because you’ll have a Sound Engineer getting you set up!
Please submit your video audition via the website www.wetransfer.com, send to jo.farley@bcpcouncil.gov.uk and make sure to include the name of your band!  
The Competition

Once the application deadline has passed, successful bands will be allocated one of two Heats and will be asked to prepare 2-3 songs (covers or originals) lasting no more than 10 minutes:

The WYSO carries forward, the traditions and ethos of orchestral training for young musicians in Wessex. The orchestra offers "the complete package"; top quality professional training on a weekly basis, lively and detailed rehearsals - equipping the WYSO team with the confidence, knowledge and knowhow to be able to successfully tackle some of the most exciting symphonic programmes in the repertoire. Combining this rehearsal approach with regular performance opportunities in professional venues - both locally and beyond, assures that this young orchestra truly stands out from the crowd.

International connections remain an important feature for the WYSO. Recently striking new "twinning" friendships with similar youth orchestras abroad, the WYSO has new projects in both the digital and live "pipelines", with international performances planned for 2025.

​As the name suggests, this ensemble welcomes the newest string players, giving these young musicians their first taste of orchestral playing. 

The New Strings rehearsals enable youngsters to begin to make use of the skills they are learning in their instrumental lessons. The focus is on playing together, listening to each other, maintaining good posture and keeping going!

It brings a vital extra purpose to their instrumental lessons and practice at home, enables these newest young musicians to meet and make new friends with the same interests.

​Wessex Apprentice Strings - Saturdays - 10.30am - 12pm
Playing level: Grades 4 - 6+ - no audition requirement

This more advanced string ensemble encourages instrumental progression, tackling music of an intermediate level. The 90 minute rehearsals allow for a more in depth look at string playing and ensemble work; focusing on rhythmic control and tone production, building stamina, concentration skills and general performance confidence - especially useful skills if considering auditioning for the Wessex Youth Symphony Orchestra. 

The Wessex Apprentice Strings also the pathway to participation in the Wessex Young Apprentice Orchestra - an intermediate symphony orchestra, running on a project to project basis, offering intermediate level musicians an introduction to full orchestral performance. ​


* Graded exams need not have been taken - the grade level simply allows us to select the appropriate ensemble for each player

Wessex Junior Wind Band (WJWB) - Saturdays - 9.30am - 10.30am
Playing level: Grades 1-4* - no audition requirement

The WJWB caters for our newest wind, brass and percussion players; giving them an all-round introduction to the wind band world. The repertoire is carefully chosen to suit the introductory level, challenging just enough to ensure progress.

The hour-long rehearsal enables these new young musicians to begin to build stamina and improve concentration and offers insights into rhythm work, breath control and listening skills as well. 

But most importantly, it offers youngsters from all over the area the opportunity to try out their new instrumental skills alongside others who are also starting out on their music-making journey, so making new friends becomes a happy extra!

Wessex Youth Wind Band (WYWB) - Saturdays - 10.45am - 12.15pm
Playing level: Grades 5-7+* - no audition requirement

​This more advanced ensemble welcomes intermediate level wind, brass and percussionists from all over the Wessex area.

The 90 minute rehearsals enable a more in depth look at repertoire, and technical aspects of wind, brass and percussion playing, tackling a wide variety of music from traditional wind band repertoire, film and show scores and some classical pieces too. 

The WYWB is also the pathway to participation in the Wessex Young Apprentice Orchestra - an intermediate symphony orchestra, running on a project to project basis, offering an introduction to full orchestral performance. 



* Graded exams need not have been taken - the grade level simply allows us to select the appropriate ensemble for each player

The recorder family offers an accessible gateway to the world of classical music-making. It is the perfect choice for a young would-be musician wanting to begin their musical journey. The instruments are affordable, light to hold and don't require as much "puff" as some of the other woodwind instruments, so can be played by the youngest students.

And whilst learning to play the instrument, our young musicians also learn to read music, all the while developing greater dexterity, listening skills, breath control, communication, concentration, and cooperation.  

It's an ideal way to introduce youngsters to the fun and discipline of playing in groups and whilst for some it may become a preliminary step to taking up orchestral instruments in the future, for others they may find that this instrument, first documented way back in the 1500s, becomes their  main instrument.

SoundStorm’s Creative Ensemble will be back in Autumn 2025. We will be offering Creative Ensemble membership to include all 7 sessions below and an additional performance opportunity.

When? The sessions will take place on Saturdays: 11th October, 8th November, 10th January, 14th February, 14th March and 9th May from 10:30am-3pm (for members and non-members) and on 13th June (rehearsal from 10:30am-3pm and concert to take place in the late afternoon, exact time tbc – for members only)

Where? Magna Academy, Ashdown Close, Poole, Dorset, BH17 8RE

What is it? The creative ensemble is led by musicians from the Bath Philharmonia Creative Learning Team, working with SoundStorm practitioners, and is open to all musicians on any instrument, including voice. All standards are welcome, from beginner to expert. It is highly subsidised thanks to Arts Council funding.

Next year, we are again offering the choice for young people to join the Creative Ensemble as members in addition to the option to attend one off sessions throughout the year. Members will automatically be given a space for all sessions but there will still be a number of spaces made available for those not wishing to sign up for membership. Sessions will continue to focus on creative music making. Members will be performing the music created throughout the year in a special concert on 13th June 2026 (further details tbc)

In order to apply for a place (as a member or non-member):

You need to be school-age, aged 10-17.
You don’t need to be able to read music; everything will be learnt by ear!
You will need to bring your instrument, including leads and amps (if electric).
You’ll also need to bring enough drinks and a packed lunch/snacks to keep you going during the day. There won’t be an opportunity to buy refreshments during the day.
 How much? £70 for membership up until end of June 2026 (members will need to commit to at least 4 sessions including the May and June sessions). £15.00 to attend one-off sessions.

We do not want financial hardship to be a barrier for young people. To find out how SoundStorm can assist, please contact Claire Butler at claire.butler@bcpcouncil.gov.uk.

For any other queries regarding the Creative Ensemble, please contact Sarah Derwish at sarah.derwish@bcpcouncil.gov.uk

13. About Opportunity:
Creative Music Clubs for Young People

B Sharp provides exciting opportunities for teenage musicians to play, create, and grow in confidence through music.
Lyme Regis Hub Jams and Bridport Jams are welcoming, creative music clubs where young people come together to make music, collaborate with others, and develop their own tracks and songs in a supportive environment.
What are B Sharp Jams?
Our Jam sessions are weekly, informal music-making sessions for young people aged 11–18 (Year 7 and up), held at The Hub in Lyme Regis and Bridport Youth & Community Centre.
Sessions are open to:
Instrumentalists
Singers and vocalists
Music producers and technicians
Young people work together to jam, experiment with ideas, and create original music — no exams, no pressure, just creativity and collaboration.
There are chances to perform throughout the year — totally optional, and always supported.
All abilities are welcome, from beginners to more experienced musicians.
A safe, supportive space
B Sharp Jams are led by experienced music practitioners who support young people to:
Build musical skills and confidence
Work as part of a group
Express themselves creatively
Try new instruments, styles, and ideas
It’s a relaxed and friendly space where young people can be themselves and enjoy making music with others who share their interests.
Inclusive and affordable
We believe every young person should be able to make music, regardless of their financial circumstances.
Sessions are affordable
Subsidised and free places are available
Please get in touch if cost might be a barrier — we’re happy to help.
Try a free taster session
New members are welcome to come along for a free taster session before signing up. It’s a great way to meet the group, try the session, and see if B Sharp Jams is the right fit.

13. About Opportunity:
Little B’s Tiny Tunes! 
A fun and supportive space for parents and babies under 12 months, every Thursday at 10am at Bridport Family Hub, starting February 27th.
Our newest group is designed especially for new parents with babies under one and offers a mix of gentle music, songs, and rhymes that are perfect for bonding with your little one.
It’s also a great opportunity to connect with other parents, share experiences, and get advice on feeding, self-care and well-being from a dedicated support worker. Because we know being a new parent can be challenging!
Free to attend, but spaces may be limited to ensure a calm and nurturing environment.
We look forward to welcoming you, please feel free to contact us with any questions, or just come along to the session.
Time: Thursdays 10.00am
Location: Bridport Family Hub 
What to Bring: Just you, and your baby!
Cost: Sessions are free.
 
How music supports parents and babies 
Research (https://breatheahr.org/research/melodies-for-mums/) shows that group music-making and singing can boost maternal mental health. Our sessions provide an inclusive and social creative activity, that provides parents with an opportunity to be immersed in an enjoyable activity and bond with their baby. There is a wealth of research that music support babies and toddlers’ development, including communication and language skills, social development and fosters a life-long love of music.

Thursdays 11.15am @ Bridport Family Hub
Autumn Term starting Thursday 18th September 2025 (term time only)

Little B’s Music Together is weekly music sessions to support new parents and carers to bond with their baby and boost wellbeing. If you are a new parent or carer of a baby or toddler (up to 3 years), and are feeling socially isolated, or experiencing feelings of stress or anxiety please join us for a music programme designed to support you and your little one. The Harbour Records/B Sharp MusicFutures programme supports young aspiring musicians (aged 16 to 25) living in Dorset, UK, to take the next steps towards a creative career. 
Participants are supported to record and release their original music on the Harbour Records Label. Through the programme they will develop their musical abilities and confidence, build their industry knowledge, develop their networks and showcase their talents through monthly industry masterclasses, networking and showcase events and individual mentoring.  
The programme runs from October 2025– July 2026. 
The programme involves: 
Masterclasses in composition, recording, production, musical skills, finance and marketing 
Networking, sharing and socials 
Individual mentoring and recording sessions 
Final showcase event 
Programme dates:
Wednesdays 7pm – 9pm
2025 – Oct 22nd, Nov 26th, Dec 10th
2026 – Jan 21st, Feb 25th, March 25th, April 22nd, May 20th, June 17th, July tba
Individual mentoring and recording sessions will be arranged around your availability.
Venue:  Lyric Theatre, Bridport, Dorset 
To apply for this year’s programme please complete our application form:
Link to Music Futures Application Form
Any queries please email info@bsharp.org.uk 
The programme is funded by Octagon Music Hub and Youth Music.

Individual sessions 
We offer one-to-one music making sessions for young people age 8 – 25, who are experiencing challenging circumstances.

13. About Opportunity:
The Brighton & East Sussex Youth Orchestra meet for weekly rehearsals during term-time, setting itself the challenge to perform great music of the classical repertoire. They have performed music by symphonies by Brahms, Beethoven and Florence Price, as well as Copland’s Appalachian Spring and Bernstein’s iconic West Side Story. They have also performed film and gaming music such as Star Wars, Batman and Horizon: Zero Dawn.

BESYO is often given the opportunity to perform at Brighton Dome as part of Brighton Festival, performing concertos including Elgar’s infamous Cello Concerto, and Florence Price’s Piano Concerto with Jeneba Kanneh-Mason. They also perform throughout Brighton & East Sussex, as well as regular tours to France, Belgium and Germany.

The orchestra’s Artistic Director is Pete Davison, composer and conductor, who is supported by a team of tutors who give their time and experience to working with the orchestra at rehearsals on Friday evenings. Auditions take place throughout the year. If you play an orchestral instrument and are Grade 6 standard or above, why not apply?

Gloucestershire Music is able to offer additional support through their Foundation Orchestras in Gloucester and Stroud – and they are FREE.

Nick Steel (Music Service School's Manager) said:  "Joining one of our Foundation Orchestras is a great way to improve musicianship and hone young musician's skills. Playing in a group or small ensemble is an essential part of developing any young musician's ability."

Foundation Orchestra sessions last for 45 - 60 minutes and are designed to improve ensemble skills through rhythm games, singing and practical music making.

Foundation Orchestra can compliment lessons that your child may be receiving at school or at home and provides a gentle introduction to your local music centre.

If you have just started playing an instrument, then join us and become a Foundation Orchestra musician.
17. About Organisation:
About Us

Gloucestershire Music is the county’s largest delivery partner for the county’s Music Education Hub ‘Make Music Gloucestershire’, which is funded by Arts Council England.

 Our Mission

is to provide high quality, accessible and enjoyable musical experiences for children and adults.  Our team is passionate about music education and making a difference to people’s lives through the power of music.  Whether playing an instrument or singing is a hobby or a career path choice we have something to offer everyone, as we provide musical ensembles for all levels of learner.
is to support schools and music educators around the county to confidently deliver music to young people in their classrooms
Delivery

 At Gloucestershire Music we...

provide schools with whole class instrumental lessons, CPD for staff, special projects, workshops, support and advice…and more
offer children and young people opportunities to play and perform together in our music groups – orchestras, wind bands, jazz groups, choirs and other ensembles, at our out-of-school hours music centres
provide instruments – from bassoons to xylophones, through our instrument hire service to anyone in the County (not just pupils)
... and run other activities including workshops, masterclasses, and more!

 

Events

We proudly organise some major music festivals for Gloucestershire:

The National Concert Band Festival – we host it’s South West and Wales Regional Festival in the county annually 
Music for Youth – we have organised a major two-day Regional Festival in Gloucestershire every March for the last 5 years and it is one of the most well attended in the country 
BBC Ten Pieces events 
County concerts – numerous concerts are held throughout the year across the county showcasing the talents of our groups.  Read about some of these on our News and Events page
Tours and Residentials – some of our members and groups take part in amazing tours abroad!  Read about our past tours here
Partnership Working

At Gloucestershire Music we want to inspire our county’s musicians and young people and therefore we continue to work closely with National Partnership Organisations and professional ensembles and artists such as the Bournemouth Symphony Orchestra, National Choir of Great Britain, Birmingham Conservatoire (LEAP Ensemble), Tasmin Little OBE, Guy Barker, Julian Bliss, Craig Mather, Band of the Bugles and the Rifles, Philip Harper, Andy Coxon, Nathanial Morrison, Keith Allan, Alexander L'Estrange, 5 Mics and Tom Davoran to name just a few! We also work with otherneighbouring Music Hubs such as South Gloucestershire and Bath and North East Somerset to collaborate on major projects such as the Music For Youth Massed Ensemble for the Schools Prom at the Royal Albert Hall!

Partnership Working

13. About Opportunity:
Five Valleys Adult Wind Band is a group specifically for adults players. Working at grade 5 and upwards, the group caters for those who would like to continue developing their playing and ensemble skills whilst at the same time enjoying the experience of being in a band.  We have a wide ranging repertoire including film, swing, and classical music and play several concerts a year.  Recently we've played in Cheltenham Town Hall, Pittville Pump Room and taken part in the National Concert Band Festival.

We are a friendly and supportive group, and you would be welcome to come and join us. Contact our Director, Jenny Steele before attending the first time - glosmusic@gloucestershire.gov.uk.

Rehearsals are at Archway School in Stroud and take place from 9:30am-11:30am on a Saturday morning, term time only. We ask that members be a minimum standard of Grade 5. Please see the calendar for further rehearsal details.

13. About Opportunity:
* A junior group encouraging the next young generation of budding jazz musicians.
* Training - This ensemble is the ideal opportunity for any young musicians keen to explore jazz music for the first time and develop fundamental musical skills and musicianship.

9. Level: Ideally Grade 6+, 3 x trial rehearsals & audition required
13. About Opportunity:
* An advanced senior group ideally for experienced youth performers, encouraging the next leading young generation of budding Jazz musicians.
* Training - This ensemble is the highest accolade for young county musicians wishing to develop and advance their Jazz individual and ensemble playing skills even further.

* Young musicians ideally grade 6 and above are encouraged to trial and audition, however the willingness to participate, listen attentively and the ability to have independent music notation reading (and sight-reading), frequent practice and improvising skills is all important. There is no expectation to be strong at all of these, however being open to trying and committing is vital to every members' and the band's success.

* Entry to the band is through attending three trial rehearsals and an audition (please see the "GYJO entry, trials and audition" menu for more information). A strong commitment in rehearsal and independent practice is pivotal to gaining the most out of the GYJO experience.

* Ideal for young musicians up to the age of 21 (anyone over 21 is eligible for Jazz2Go)
* The band is directed by Peter Martin.
* GYJO learn and perform a diverse range of repertoire with an emphasis on broad variety, challenge and developing advanced listening, ensemble, improvisation and other jazz skills (eg. theoretical knowledge of chords, chord symbols and sequences / scales and modes / performing in different genres and styles etc.).

* Since 2006 the band have had the opportunity for coaching that allows band-members' arrangements and compositions to be developed, performed, recorded and fully showcased.  

* Performances - GYJO is involved in our GM Presents concerts, our annual GJL concerts and typically performs in youth and jazz festivals each academic year (eg. NCBF, Music for Youth regional and national festivals, Cheltenham Jazz festival, Upton Jazz Festival, Jazz Stroud). On some occasions they are involved in GYJO specific concerts or invited to be involved in other performance opportunities eg. Gloucestershire Motor Show, Churchdown Community Centre, Rotary Club Carol Concert 2023 and 2024. In November 2016 GYJO had the privilege to perform at the Music For Youth Proms at the Royal Albert Hall. 

* After GYJO?  Some members typically move onto jobs / apprenticeships and can join our Jazz2Go group once age 22. GYJO members who go to university / college courses have kept us up-to-date on further education / community big-bands and ensembles they join and of course create, lead and tour themselves!

* Music graduates - Many ex-GYJO players have gained places on Music under and post graduate degree courses (including Royal Birmingham Conservatoire, Royal Welsh College of Music and Drama, Trinity Laban Conservatoire, Leeds College of Music, Royal Northern College of Music, Royal Academy of Music, Liverpool University, Oxford University, Cambridge University and Berklee College of Music [Boston, USA]). Some ex-GYJO players are now professional musicians and music educators too.

13. About Opportunity:
Gloucestershire Youth Orchestra was formed in 1951 and is one of the county music groups run by Gloucestershire Music.

The orchestra give as many as six concerts a year, performing programmes that range from the 18th century to the present day. GYO have been awarded national recognition for their performances by attending Music for Youth’s National Festival as well as special recognition for their innovative performances.

Membership of GYO is by audition or teacher nomination, please contact us if you want to be considered for a place. Wind, brass and percussion players need to be at around a grade 7+ standard and string players around grade 6+. A positive attitude and commitment to rehearsals and concerts is required. 

Members are expected to attend at least 80% of rehearsals and give a firm commitment to all concert engagements. Absences from rehearsals should be communicated by emailing glosmusic@gloucestershire.gov.uk

 

Statement for all County Music Groups regarding membership

Grades are only a guide to the level required.  More important is a strong commitment and positive attitude alongside good sight reading and general musicianship skills. Teachers may recommend students for a specific group but the Musical Director has the final decision to whether a student is ready for entry into an ensemble.  Once you have gained a place in one of the county music groups, you will be required to maintain a high percentage of attendance at rehearsals and concerts.  A place within an ensemble may be withdrawn if commitment is deemed unsatisfactory. You can audition and join at any point throughout the year.  If a group is preparing for a concert or festival, you may be asked to wait a few weeks before joining.

13. About Opportunity:
Gloucestershire Youth String Orchestra is a national award winning string orchestra that allows young string players (grade 5 +) the opportunity to develop their ensemble skills and to tackle challenging repertoire. Sessions are presented and delivered in a fun and inclusive manner.

Gloucestershire Youth String Orchestra (GYSO) is the premier youth string orchestra in Gloucestershire consisting of 60+ players.

This year, GYSO will perform at the Royal Albert Hall, Pittville Pump Rooms, Cheltenham Town Hall, St Mary's Church Painswick, Tetbury Goods Shed, Tewkesbury Abbey and will tour Germany in August 2026

The age range is predominantly 11 - 18 and above, although younger members are always welcome.

If you would like to join GYSO, you will need to be a minimum grade 5 level on the violin, viola, cello or double bass. Grades are only a guide to the level required, more important is a strong commitment and positive attitude alongside good sight reading and general musicianship skills.

GYSO will provide valuable ensemble experience for young string players . As a member of GYSO they may also qualify for Gloucestershire Youth Orchestra (GYO). GYO rehearse monthly as a course based activity.

Teachers may recommend students for a specific group but the Musical Director/Orchestral Manager has the final decision to whether a student is ready for entry into an ensemble.

13. About Opportunity:
GYWO was formed in 1981 and is the county’s multi-platinum award-winning ensemble, regarded as one of the finest youth concert bands in Great Britain. The band has represented Gloucestershire in the National Concert Bands Festival at the Royal Northern College of Music as well as receiving an invitation to perform at the National Festival of Music for Youth at Symphony Hall.

 

GYWO perform regularly throughout year providing great musical experiences for the students. GYWO can also boast numerous tours of Belgium, Lithuania, Germany and, most recently, a very successful six-day tour of Spain in 2022.

 

Emphasis is always on quality music making, through a wide range of musical genre and styles. If you want to perform music Elgar to E.T. then do get in touch at glosmusic@gloucestershire.gov.uk and come along on a Thursday.

Standard

We recommend you are at least of Grade 5+ standard to join GYWO, but this doesn't mean you need to have taken the exams. We welcome all wind, brass and percussion instruments.

Delivery

 At Gloucestershire Music we...

provide schools with whole class instrumental lessons, CPD for staff, special projects, workshops, support and advice…and more
offer children and young people opportunities to play and perform together in our music groups – orchestras, wind bands, jazz groups, choirs and other ensembles, at our out-of-school hours music centres
provide instruments – from bassoons to xylophones, through our instrument hire service to anyone in the County (not just pupils)
... and run other activities including workshops, masterclasses, and more!

 

Events

We proudly organise some major music festivals for Gloucestershire:

The National Concert Band Festival – we host it’s South West and Wales Regional Festival in the county annually 
Music for Youth – we have organised a major two-day Regional Festival in Gloucestershire every March for the last 5 years and it is one of the most well attended in the country 
BBC Ten Pieces events 
County concerts – numerous concerts are held throughout the year across the county showcasing the talents of our groups.  Read about some of these on our News and Events page
Tours and Residentials – some of our members and groups take part in amazing tours abroad!  Read about our past tours here
Partnership Working

At Gloucestershire Music we want to inspire our county’s musicians and young people and therefore we continue to work closely with National Partnership Organisations and professional ensembles and artists such as the Bournemouth Symphony Orchestra, National Choir of Great Britain, Birmingham Conservatoire (LEAP Ensemble), Tasmin Little OBE, Guy Barker, Julian Bliss, Craig Mather, Band of the Bugles and the Rifles, Philip Harper, Andy Coxon, Nathanial Morrison, Keith Allan, Alexander L'Estrange, 5 Mics and Tom Davoran to name just a few! We also work with otherneighbouring Music Hubs such as South Gloucestershire and Bath and North East Somerset to collaborate on major projects such as the Music For Youth Massed Ensemble for the Schools Prom at the Royal Albert Hall!

The fund was established in 1977 following the death in 1974 of Dorothy Grinstead who was a much- loved piano teacher and musician in Croydon.  A board of Trustees was formed to administer the fund to assist young Croydon musicians.

How can the fund help you?
Help with purchasing a new instrument

Help with tuition costs

Help with course costs

Criteria for applying
You must be, or have been, a student at a Croydon School/Academy for a substantial part of your secondary education, or currently in Year 6 at a Croydon Primary School/Academy OR be a Croydon resident

You should be Grade 5 standard minimum in your instrument

You must be a member of an ensemble/orchestra / music group or choir

You must supply references from your instrumental teacher and school

You will need to provide a reference or exam results to show how you have progressed with your instrument

You must be under 26 years of age

13. About Opportunity:
For young people aged 11 to 19 years old (up to 24 years old for D/deaf and disabled young people)

The Ensemble meets regularly from September to July and performs at exciting events across London and beyond. In the past year, LYFE has performed at Cecil Sharp House, Folk By The Oak festival, Trafalgar Square, and Greenwich Park. 

LYFE is open to confident musicians of any instrument. This means that you should be able to play your instrument independently and confidently, but do not need to have passed any particular grade. All music is taught by ear and no previous experience of folk music is needed.  

London Youth Folk Ensemble is an annual commitment and places are offered after you attend our Sampler session at the start of the Autumn term.

Taking part can contribute to an Arts Award Bronze or Silver Award.

How do I join?
To join the London Youth Folk Ensemble you need to: 

Be 11 to 19 years old (up to up to 24 year olds for D/deaf and disabled young people)
Have a good grasp of technique on your instrument and be able to play tunes confidently
Have some experience of learning and playing tunes by ear
Have some experience of playing your instrument in a group (eg band, ensemble, orchestra)
Demonstrate passion for folk music and commitment to the Ensemble
Attend a free trial to meet the tutors, to gain a taste of playing with the ensemble and for us to see if you are ready to join the ensemble 
 

If you would like to attend a sampler session, please get in touch at education@efdss.org

 

London Youth Folk Ensemble is an annual commitment.



 

Learning by ear means I have gained a more intuitive and collaborative approach to music which is really fun and enables me to be creative

Fees
Fees will be payable after your taster session if you decide you would like to join the Ensemble for the full academic year.  

Annual LYFE fee: £350 | £275 concessions

You are eligible for the concessionary rate if:

You are in receipt of free school meals
You are a looked-after or displaced young person
Your parent/guardian is in receipt of an Income-Based Benefit (such as Universal Credit)
To enquire about the concessionary rate, please contact education@efdss.org

Bursary Places

We want more young people from a wider range of backgrounds and economic circumstances to benefit from joining London Youth Folk Ensemble.

To support this, we have set aside a limited number of bursary places (partial or full bursaries) to enable keen young musicians who would not otherwise be able to afford the concessionary fees for the Ensemble.

To find out more about bursaries, please contact education@efdss.org

We wish to encourage progression from earliest grassroots activity in schools and community settings on to lifelong engagement with folk music – as participants and professionals.

The National Youth Folk Ensemble celebrates excellence in youth folk music, bringing together talented young folk musicians from across England to create and perform inspiring new arrangements of folk music.

With young musicians at the heart of the programme, the National Youth Folk Ensemble aims to nurture their talents, aspirations and creativity, enabling them to develop into the folk performers, educators and leaders of the future.

These talented musicians receive expert tuition and guidance from a team of leading folk artist-educators, working with the Ensemble’s Artistic Director (2023–2026), Jo Freya.

Join us for fortnightly creative, inclusive, multi-sensory music and dance workshops at Cecil Sharp House on Sunday afternoons.

Young people will sing, dance, and play folk music from a range of different heritages in creative, inclusive, and multi-sensory sessions, including opportunities for participants to perform the songs and dances they have learnt to friends and family!

The sessions are led by folk artists who are experienced working with learning disabled young people with complex needs.

 At Folk Unlimited you can:

Learn to sing, dance, and play folk music from a range of different heritages
Participate in creative, inclusive, and multi-sensory sessions
Learn and explore new instruments and accessible technology
Learn new skills with fantastic tutors
Make new friends
 

Who is it for?
The workshops are for learning disabled young people aged 12 to 30, who live or go to school/college in one of the John Lyon's Charity boroughs:

Barnet
Brent
Camden
City of London
City of Westminster
Ealing
Hammersmith & Fulham
Harrow
Kensington & Chelsea
You do not need any experience of playing an instrument or folk music to take part in Folk Unlimited. The workshops are open to all abilities and levels of experience and are great fun!

 

When are the workshops?
Sundays, 2.30pm to 4.30pm, at Primrose Hill Community Library

Autumn Term 2025: 21 September | 5, 19 October | 2, 16, 30 November

Term fee: £39 per term (advance booking required)

Please note that this is a single booking for the whole term.
