The Hackney Borough Youth Orchestra is a KS3+ ensemble for Grade 5 + standard young musicians.  It brings together the most advanced players from across the borough and provides them with an opportunity to play challenging repertoire, introducing them to a diverse range musical genres and composers from around the world, supported by an exceptional team of tutors.  HBYO is a progression route for Hackney Training Orchestra players, though eligible players are also welcome to apply directly to join HBYO.   The orchestra performs regularly, and there are many opportunities to work alongside other HMS Ensembles and Partner Organisations.  Recent projects include A World Music Odyssey with the Grand Union Orchestra and Everyone’s Climbing Tree, and the Take Note project with the London Symphony Orchestra. HBYO players are also frequently nominated for special projects e.g. NYO Inspire courses, the LSO’s East London Academy and opportunities to take part in special events such as the LSO’s annual open air concert in Trafalgar Square.

Hackney Borough Youth Orchestra is open to players in Year 7 and above
Applicants must be of Grade 5 standard or higher. Applicants need not necessarily have taken a Grade 5 exam but should be able to play and sight-read to the level expected for Grade 5 players
Exceptions are very occasionally made to the minimum standard / age requirements where we have a shortage of particular orchestral forces, or due to other exceptional circumstances
Applicants will be invited to attend a friendly and informal audition / trial session
Applicants must either live in the borough of Hackney or attend a Hackney school
HBYO may also accept pupils from neighbouring boroughs, please get in touch for more information

Music Explorers is an adventure in music for Reception and Year 1 aged children (age 4-6). In each 45-minute session children are invited to explore, setting out on an adventure. This could be a new way of looking at music, an instrument or a guest musician. At the heart of the programme is a child led ethos.

Our sessions will foster independence and a range of ways of participating; explore rich variety of songs, rhymes, and music games; encourage self-initiated musical play with a wide range of music of different backgrounds and traditions.

Facilitators will tune in to the children’s ways of being musical; respond and play creatively with children in music allowing them to retain the initiative; devise ways of allowing children to continue to explore their ideas with musical instruments, the voice and body.

Children will be given to opportunity to sing and perform spontaneously in a range of different ways; repeat, transform, combine and develop their own and borrowed musical ideas into increasingly complex structure. Over time children will be able to control and coordinate their whole body and coordinate their music ideas with other.

10. Instruments: Piccolo/Flute, Clarinet, Oboe, Cor Anglais, Bassoon, Trumpet, Trombone, Tuba, Horns, Percussion, Euphonium, Saxophone
13. About Opportunity:
The Kingston and Malden Scout and Guide Band was formed in 1950 and is based in the Royal Borough of Kingston upon Thames in Surrey. Its members come from Scout Groups and Guide units across the local area.

The Band was awarded Kingston’s Borough Colours in 1997 in recognition for the work the Band does to “fly the flag” for its home town and holds the highest Scout Association A grading.

Since it’s earliest days as a drum and bugle band, Kingston and Malden has progressed as an engaging activity for young musicians decade by decade. There have been five consecutive performances within the City of London’s Lord Mayor’s Show in recent years and three summer expeditions to Guernsey, taking part in the Battle of Flowers festivities. More recently the band has had the chance to play at the House of Commons, a Buckingham Palace Garden Party, the National Scout St George’s Parade at Windsor Castle and the London 2012 Olympic road cycle race. A constantly evolving musical repertoire means that the band often now plays in a concert formation as well as the more traditional marching band style.

The Band strikes a balance in what it does and what is expected from its young people. At present, over 60 members are on the books and these range from Brownies and Cub Scouts aged 8, to Scouts, Guides, Rangers, Explorer Scouts and young leaders within both associations.

Membership of the band is a parallel activity to regular Scouting and Guiding activities so members have to spread their time accordingly, but it is policy for the youngest or newest recruit to be fully included in all engagements and activities as soon as possible and this has proven to be a winning formula for the Band.

Many of the Band’s members progress into leadership roles. Older members also take time to teach music to their younger colleagues. For a nominal monthly subscription, members of the Band get this tuition, a uniform and the loan of an instrument.

The last few years have witnessed the Band developing a great, all round sound, with an even greater array of instrumentation. The Band’s repertoire is extensive and suits all tastes. Music from the world of stage and screen and the movies sits alongside arrangements of well known pop hits from past and present. And, as a marching band, there are naturally a good number of march pieces available! Some examples include:

• Frank Sinatra’s My Way
• It Don’t Mean a Thing if you Ain’t Got That Swing
• All I Ask of You – from Phantom of the Opera
• Children of Sanchez – by Chuck Mangione
• 633 Squadron
• Royal Air Force March Past
• The Incredibles – soundtrack to the Disney Pixar animation film
• Can’t Take My Eyes off You – sung by Andy Williams
• Favourite Things – from the Sound of Music
• The Great Escape

13. About Opportunity:
Young Voices is MMF’s junior choir for children aged 7-12 (Years 3-7). The aim of the group is to develop general musicianship skills and ensemble experience through use of the voice and provides an ideal introduction for the young or ‘novice’ musician.

Members learn how to control and expand their vocal pitch and dynamic range, to pitch-match (sing in tune), the importance of good posture and clear diction, to sing in two or more parts and to use their voices expressively, reflecting the style and context of a wide variety of songs. General Musicianship activities help to foster an understanding of pulse, metre, rhythm, phrase, musical structure, pitch and pitch-patterns (scales) and develop ‘thinking voice’. The group performs publicly at the end of each term as part of MMF’s junior events and at many other external venues and events.

Rehearsals take place in St John's Church, High Path (next door to Merton Abbey Primary School SW19 2JY) and led by Soo Bishop.

13. About Opportunity:
The Bridging Sounds Orchestra is Newham Music’s flagship ensemble which brings together the vibrant and diverse communities of Newham and celebrates their musical traditions in a brilliant fusion of sounds. The BSO builds bridges between the talented young musicians from all backgrounds through the power of learning and creating music that truly represents them.

Originally the Roma Bridging Sounds Orchestra, the project was delivered in partnership with the Roma Support Group as an initiative to train young, talented Roma musicians in a culturally diverse and inclusive environment while exploring the rich musical influences from across Eastern Europe. Musicians in the new Bridging Sounds Orchestra now have the opportunity to explore and experiment with these as well as many other world-music influences, particularly those represented here in Newham.

The Bridging Sounds Orchestra have been honoured to perform for the National Holocaust Memorial Day ceremony and at the Royal Albert Hall. Their first full-length album was recorded at the world-famous Abbey Road Studios and released in 2018.

13. About Opportunity:
Surbiton Royal British Legion Youth Marching Band is a non profit making organisation set up in 1965 for young people between the ages of 8-21. All tuition, instruments and uniform are provided by the band which runs along the same lines as H.M. forces.
 

 

All young people are welcome, regardless of musical experience, and the band only charge a nominal fee, thus enabling many young people who otherwise would not have the opportunity to learn to play a musical instrument to do so.

The Surbiton Royal British Legion Youth Academy Band was set up in 2024. The band aims to give young people with none or little musical experience the chance to learn to read and play music, aswell as become familiar with other aspects of a Marching Band such as drill practice. 

Where as our main band recruits between the ages of 8-21, our Academy Band takes on those from age 7 upwards. The band hopes to help grow both music and social skills where young people from all different backgrounds, but with similar interests, can engage in both social and musical activities 
Surbiton Royal British Legion Youth Marching Band is a non profit making organisation set up in 1965 for young people between the ages of 8-21. All tuition, instruments and uniform are provided by the band which runs along the same lines as H.M. forces.
 

 

All young people are welcome, regardless of musical experience, and the band only charge a nominal fee, thus enabling many young people who otherwise would not have the opportunity to learn to play a musical instrument to do so.

More Music Makers is a class for children in Years 2, 3 or 4 who may have missed out on the opportunity to attend Music Kindergarten or Music Makers. They learn aural skills, musical literacy, transcription, composition, improvisation, ensemble skills and listening with understanding. These classes will enhance the musical learning of those children who are already learning an instrument while also being suitable for children who have had little formal music training

Classes use a range of percussion instruments, particularly xylophones and glockenspiels (which serve as a good introduction or accompaniment to learning the piano).

Classes for 5 to 7 year olds have been running for a number of years at the John Savage Centre, and due to popular demand they are now available on both Wednesday and Thursday evenings. Children learn through singing, moving, listening and playing a variety of tuned and un-tuned percussion instruments. These classes provide an excellent musical foundation where children can develop their singing voice, inner ear and the fine motor skills required to play a musical instrument. In the second year of the programme, children also learn to read music, equipping them to begin the recorder the following year. In Music Kindergarten and Music Makers, children develop:

Their ability to play music with awareness of others
A sense of beat through pulse/rhythmic activities
A sense of pitch through singing and playing pitched percussion
Self-confidence through “turn taking” activities and performing
Their ability to listen and respond to recorded and live music
Their ability to make musical choices/decisions
An understanding of simple music notation
A positive attitude to music and music making
Please be aware that parents/carers are expected to attend the class with their child and take an active part in supporting their child’s learning.

The gamelan is an orchestra of bronze gongs and percussion instruments from the Indonesian islands of Java and Bali. The style of music is cyclical and produces a uniquely rich and multi-layered texture. Pieces are learned aurally using numbers, and parts vary in difficulty. Gamelan performance is a communal activity in which no single musician stands out or acts as leader. Everyone works together as a team, learning to play all the different instruments in the orchestra. No previous gamelan or musical experience is required to join this inclusive ensemble. Playing in a gamelan group is fun, and helps develop deep listening, teamwork and coordination skills, as well as providing the opportunity to experience the rich legacy of this Javanese musical tradition.

Catering for children from birth to 4 years of age, these fun classes for families are a wonderful way for your child to begin their musical journey.

The classes include songs, rhymes, movement activities (such as rocking, bouncing and dancing) and a range of age-appropriate props and percussion instruments.

Each term is carefully planned to ensure variety, progression and adequate repetition. In addition to this, the toddler classes have different themes each term such as the weather, teddy bears, food, jungle animals.

Families are encouraged to continue the fun activities with their children at home in order to provide the repetition needed at this stage of their child’s development. To facilitate this, we provide the lyrics for the songs and rhymes as well as audio tracks to sing and dance along to at home.

Mini Musician classes are designed to contribute to all areas of your child’s development:

Communication and language: age-appropriate songs and rhymes
Personal, social and emotional development: interacting with other children and adults and exploring emotions through music
Physical development: a range of movement activities using props and instruments which help develop coordination, fine and gross motor skills and balance.
Literacy: developing the ability to copy rhythmic and melodic patterns aurally which will then lead to reading music notation in Music Kindergarten.
Mathematics: counting songs, rhymes and movement activities
Understanding the world: each term has a few different themes with props and pictures helping create context and develop understanding
expressive arts and design – responding to music and encouraging creativity
Mini Musician classes are capped at 16 families per class in order that your child can focus and receive the attention they deserve. With different songs and activities each term, there is always something new and fun!

The Technical Academy unlocks hidden career opportunities in technical roles in live events and theatre. We want to encourage new people – especially those from under-represented communities – to gain valuable experience and join us working in technical production.

Run by the Southbank Centre, the Technical Academy is delivered in partnership with The Albany, Association of British Theatre Technicians (ABTT), ATG, Factory International, Livewire Productions, National Theatre, Omnii Collective, Roundhouse, Royal Albert Hall, RNSS and The Production House.

The Technical Academy is hands-on production training for people with little or no experience, taught by the technicians who do it every day. If you’re 18+ and live in London, our three-week training programme could be your next step towards a brand new career. Everyone is welcome.

Find out about the range of work that happens backstage in creative venues – from building stages, rigging lights and setting up drum kits, to designing sound, lighting and audio visual effects. The Technical Academy is your chance to learn the basics, see how technicians work, and discover the need-to-knows for getting work in the industry.

The Orchestras
Founded in 1944, Stoneleigh Youth Orchestra is one of the oldest youth orchestras in the UK. Based in Wimbledon, London, we currently have over 160 players aged from 8 to 18, drawn from around 80 different schools all over Surrey and south and west London.

Stoneleigh comprises two orchestras: the Main Orchestra, aimed at musicians who are the equivalent of Grade 7 standard or above, and the Training Orchestra, designed for less experienced musicians who are at least Grade 4 standard. The orchestras rehearse in Wimbledon on Sunday afternoons during school term time and perform three concerts a year in some of the best concert halls in London and Surrey. Recent venues include Cadogan Hall, St John’s Smith Square, Dorking Halls, and the Royal College of Music. The Main Orchestra also undertakes a week-long tour in Europe each July.

Although you don’t need to be able to read music to join our choir, it is helpful if you have some sight-reading experience.  

What happens in an audition? 

Our informal auditions are an opportunity for us to learn more about you and your voice and see where you might fit in our choir. During the 10-minute audition, you will be asked to sing one of your favourite songs. You can sing it a cappella (unaccompanied) or with a backing track, if you have one. After you have sung your song, we will ask you to sing a few vocal warm-ups to check your range and ask you about your previous singing experiences. We may also ask you to do some short sight-reading, depending on experience.

Kingston Music Network
Learn more about this group of professionals from education and the music industry who meet to collaborate, foster musical growth and encourage musical opportunities for young people in Kingston.

Vision
The Kingston Music Network aims to be a proactive, collaborative body dedicated to fostering musical growth and inspiration among young people in Kingston. Its core purpose is to:

Grow and Inspire Music Making: Actively promote and develop musical opportunities for children and young people across the borough.
Facilitate Action: Move beyond discussion to implement concrete projects and initiatives.
Foster Collaboration: Connect individuals and organisations with shared passions and diverse skill sets to work in partnership.
Amplify Impact: Enhance the reach and effectiveness of existing and new music initiatives.
Structure
The Kingston Music Network will operate with a flexible, project-based structure designed to maximise impact and engagement:

Network Meetings: The whole Network will meet in July and December to feedback progress and decide on annual focus topics.
Sub-Groups: The network will be divided into three sub-groups. Each sub-group will focus on a specific topic decided by the Network.
Membership
The Kingston Music Network will comprise:

Core Members: Individuals from education, music and wider arts organisations who are committed to the network's aims and action-oriented objectives.
Invited Experts: Experts will be proactively invited to join specific sub-groups, bringing specialised knowledge and skills to address particular topics.
Community Engagement: The network will actively seek to involve parents and other untapped community resources who are passionate about supporting music in schools, recognising the time and financial constraints faced by music leads.
Topics for 2025 to 2026
The network will initially focus its efforts around three critical themes, which will guide the work of its sub-groups. These themes will be decided by the Network at its July meeting each year. In 2025/26, the Network has decided to focus on the following topics:

Inclusivity: Ensuring music education and participation are accessible to all young people, including those in SEND and mainstream schools, and exploring untapped community resources.
Ensembles: Developing and promoting opportunities for young people to participate in musical ensembles of all genres and for all abilities, especially those run by KMS. Looking at routes into ensembles, promotion by tutors and schools and analysis of pupil groups who prefer not to join ensembles.
Roots into Industry: Creating pathways and connections for young musicians to explore careers and opportunities within the music industry.

Tomorrow’s Warriors has built a reputation as the hippest young jazz collective in the country and has long been acclaimed for spawning the most exciting young jazz musicians. Formed in September 2009, the young musicians of the Tomorrow’s Warriors Big Band who are generally aged between 15-25, have been honing their skills and creating a unique vibe and a shared fresh approach to jazz and contemporary music. Their speciality is performing not only big band arrangements of jazz standards but also hip new arrangements of their favourite contemporary tunes and new original works by our associated artists.

Tomorrow’s Warriors Big Band is always keen to engage in exciting artistic collaborations with other music, dance, poetry and literature, and visual arts groups, so do get in touch if you are interested in collaborating with us.

Tomorrow’s Warriors Frontline is a formidable 8-piece line-up of some of our finest young Warriors musicians, championing the role of women in jazz. Membership of the ensemble is by invitation only.

Frontline is one of Tomorrow’s Warriors’ flagship development groups under the tutelage and mentorship of Romarna Campbell and Dr Gary Crosby OBE.

With regular performance opportunities, Frontline’s powerful live show draws inspiration from the jazz greats and blends a contemporary vibe with elements of jazz-fusion, funk, and R&B. Featuring a mix of jazz standards and their own compositions, Frontline channels the influences of Hank Mobley, Esperanza Spalding, Thelonious Monk, Wayne Shorter, Nérija and many more.

Get in touch for bookings and/or further information.

Whilst all our Junior members will have opportunities to participate in public performances at various points throughout the year, those with more advanced skills may be invited to join our Junior Band.

As a member of our Junior Band, young musicians have an opportunity to work more intensively within a small ensemble, developing ensemble/soloing/band leading skills mentored by a professional jazz musician in our artistic team. Band members also have additional performance opportunities, for example in the Imagine Children’s Festival at Southbank Centre, in the Music For Youth Festival, and in a variety of community arts and music festivals.

Our Junior Band never fails to wow audiences with their confidence, skill and passion for jazz music. And, as these budding stars progress, they may be invited to join our Youth Warriors Development Group, one of our Youth Ensembles, and possibly the Tomorrow’s Warriors Youth Orchestra.

13. About Opportunity:
Each of our cohorts reveals a new generation of rising stars. This is what makes Tomorrow’s Warriors so exciting!

Over the course of your participation in our programmes, and as part of your progression, you may be invited with other members to form one of our core TW ensembles or to join one of our existing bands.

Since inception, we have been proud and privileged to have brought together some of the most fantastic young musicians in the UK, helping them to develop, launch and establish award-winning ensembles.

Our support in the infancy stage of their careers has helped fast-track the careers of some notable artists and bands – Gary Crosby’s Nu Troop, J-Life, Denys Baptiste Quartet, Robert Mitchell’s Panacea, Soweto Kinch Quartet, Andrew McCormack Trio, Abram Wilson Sextet, Empirical, Rhythmica and, more recently, Ezra and Nérija to name just a few! – all of whom have brought diversity and richness to our cultural life in the UK and, in many cases, the international jazz scene.

Tomorrow’s Warriors StringTing is the latest addition to the Tomorrow’s Warriors family of flagship ensembles and is focused around some of our best young string players drawn from our JazzStrings development programme.

Members of Tomorrow’s Warriors StringTing meet on a regular basis with their mentor – jazz double bassist and Tomorrow’s Warriors artistic director, Dr Gary Crosby OBE – to work on a wide range of jazz repertoire for performance in a variety of settings, from public concerts and showcases to private functions.  In addition to performances as a distinct ensemble, members also have opportunities to work with our associated professional ensembles, such as Nu Civilisation Orchestra and Jazz Jamaica All Stars.

Membership of Tomorrow’s Warriors StringTing is by invitation only.

Interested in having Tomorrow’s Warriors StringTing perform at your event? Then do please get in touch!

Our music courses for babies and toddlers are a fun way for parents and children to connect and explore music together.

Taught by professional musicians and Early Years Music Specialists our progressive curriculum uses a variety of musical activities to lay a foundation for future musicality and to helping children to feel more secure and confident when coping with new experiences.

Singing and movement are always at the heart of every session. As repetition is very important to children at this stage, the classes have a familiar framework each week but will always include some new challenge. Daily routines such as nappy changing, bath time, meal times, waking, sleeping and even tidying up toys are more enjoyable if done through singing or playing musical instruments and dancing.

Kingston Music Network
Learn more about this group of professionals from education and the music industry who meet to collaborate, foster musical growth and encourage musical opportunities for young people in Kingston.

Vision
The Kingston Music Network aims to be a proactive, collaborative body dedicated to fostering musical growth and inspiration among young people in Kingston. Its core purpose is to:

Grow and Inspire Music Making: Actively promote and develop musical opportunities for children and young people across the borough.
Facilitate Action: Move beyond discussion to implement concrete projects and initiatives.
Foster Collaboration: Connect individuals and organisations with shared passions and diverse skill sets to work in partnership.
Amplify Impact: Enhance the reach and effectiveness of existing and new music initiatives.
Structure
The Kingston Music Network will operate with a flexible, project-based structure designed to maximise impact and engagement:

Network Meetings: The whole Network will meet in July and December to feedback progress and decide on annual focus topics.
Sub-Groups: The network will be divided into three sub-groups. Each sub-group will focus on a specific topic decided by the Network.
Membership
The Kingston Music Network will comprise:

Core Members: Individuals from education, music and wider arts organisations who are committed to the network's aims and action-oriented objectives.
Invited Experts: Experts will be proactively invited to join specific sub-groups, bringing specialised knowledge and skills to address particular topics.
Community Engagement: The network will actively seek to involve parents and other untapped community resources who are passionate about supporting music in schools, recognising the time and financial constraints faced by music leads.
Topics for 2025 to 2026
The network will initially focus its efforts around three critical themes, which will guide the work of its sub-groups. These themes will be decided by the Network at its July meeting each year. In 2025/26, the Network has decided to focus on the following topics:

Inclusivity: Ensuring music education and participation are accessible to all young people, including those in SEND and mainstream schools, and exploring untapped community resources.
Ensembles: Developing and promoting opportunities for young people to participate in musical ensembles of all genres and for all abilities, especially those run by KMS. Looking at routes into ensembles, promotion by tutors and schools and analysis of pupil groups who prefer not to join ensembles.
Roots into Industry: Creating pathways and connections for young musicians to explore careers and opportunities within the music industry.
The course is all about giving young people access to advanced production equipment, giving them the skills and confidence to create their own music and begin their own creative journey in music.

13. About Opportunity:
With thanks to funding from Arts Council England and Portsmouth Creates, The BASE at Portsmouth Guildhall is excited to partner with SoCo Music Project to launch the Creative Leaders Programme- a free workforce development and support programme for young creative practitioners aged 18-30, in Portsmouth. 

Whether you’re just starting out or looking to grow your creative practice into something bigger, this programme will help you develop the skills, confidence and connections to lead inspiring creative community workshops.

Who Is It For?
This programme is open to anyone aged 18-30 with a creative practice- you don’t need to be teaching, employed full-time, or already earning from your art. Examples of creative practice could be: musician (all genres), spoken word/ poetry artist, digital artist, visual artist, performer, writer, sound engineer, Music production, arts facilitation. This list is not exclusive!

What You’ll Get: 
Professional training in Creative Facilitation and group delivery, Safeguarding, Trauma-Informed Practice and Project Evaluation
Opportunities to shadow experienced artists and work with leading arts organisations in Portsmouth
Space to connect with likeminded creatives and grow your professional network
One-to-one coaching and group check-ins
Explore both the potential of life as a creative, and the steps to take to start making it work for you- whether that’s freelance projects, education, community work, or arts heritage.

13. About Opportunity:
A night to show off your work-in-progress tracks.
 
PLAYBACK is an evening to test out your latest track, give and receive feedback and meet new people. Whether you just need advice on the ending, the mix, or the lyrics, or if it’s a rough idea and you don’t know where to take it…  Meet with like-minded musicians and receive constructive feedback on how to take your music to the next level, and share your knowledge in return.

The evening is hosted by The BASE Guildhall and Liam Quinn. Liam is a composer and sound designer working music for media with 15 years of experience.

13. About Opportunity:
The FolkActive Youth Ensemble (FAYE) is open to musicians aged 11-19 who want to develop their folk music skills in a welcoming and inclusive environment. You’ll receive expert tuition and grow as a musician while having fun, learning to improvise harmonies, compose tunes, and play by ear. You’ll explore folk techniques, including common modes and tune types, enhance your instrumental technique and musicianship, and gain skills in arranging music and performing with confidence.

It’s also a chance to make friends and connect with like-minded people! The ensemble offers plenty of social opportunities to build relationships.

You’ll need your own acoustic instrument and the confidence to hold a tune or musical idea without sheet music.

The ensemble is led by Chris Nichols, educator and founder of the folk band Threepenny Bit.

13. About Opportunity: Beginners classes in carnatic music, a form of Indian classical music with origins in Southern India. Vocal singing lessons suitable for ages 7+

Students learn different traditional and scientific methods to enhance the tonal quality of voice, its pitch and projection.

Each singing and Harmonium for mixed abilities lesson begins with vocal exercises to strengthen and improve the quality of voice. The students would learn different forms of Indian music such as classical music, different ragas and they can learn to sing different tunes of Bollywood music. In addition, the students also learn basics of playing Harmonium. The emphasis of the classes is to improve and develop vocal skills and to give an insight into Indian and Bollywood music.

The Singing and Harmonium – mixed abilities class is led by Pooja Angra. Pooja has over 20 years of experience in teaching Indian Music. She has been teaching at Art Asia for last 15 years. Pooja has held several workshops at schools, colleges, music centres and music festivals. She has also been actively involved in designing and delivering several cross-generational and cross-cultural educational and artistic projects.

Pooja’s teaching techniques and range include: 

Motivational approach
Knowledge dissemination to a wide range of age groups
Periodic evaluation through series of tests
Participative curriculum through direct exposure to stage shows
Unique display of Indian music on music instruments like Violin, Saxophone, Keyboard and Guitar
Training for improved voice
Lessons on Hindustani classical, Semi-classical, Bollywood, Ghazals, Bhajans, Shabads, Sufi and Folk songs.

13. About Opportunity:
Junior Choir is a wonderful opportunity to learn music (theory and practice), contribute month by month to the services at St Michael’s, and have a lot of fun along the way. All children in year 3 and above are most welcome to join – no prior musical experience is needed.

Click here to see a review of a typical Junior Choir year…

The link above shows some of the extra optional activities we do such as: cathedral visits, a carol concert at the Leonardo Hotel, barbecues, training courses, and more.

Training


Our Junior Choir gives children the opportunity to be part of the centuries-old tradition of English church music that is envied the world over. Choristers receive a thorough training in music, with free singing and music theory tuition following the RSCM’s Voice for Life training scheme. 

Andrew Hanley (St Michael’s Director of Music) is a professional musician with comprehensive knowledge of choral music, and Rebecca Aspin and Paul Kuzan, who lead the juniors’ training programme, are experienced musicians, choristers and teachers of music. They are helped by a team of scholars and other senior choir members.

Everyone involved in training our younger singers is checked and supervised in accordance with the parish’s safeguarding policy.

Click to find out more about RSCM’s “Voice for Life”

What does it cost?


Membership is absolutely free! From time to time outings or trips may be organised which may have an associated cost, but we try to subsidise these where possible, and we often get group rates. We also run occasional fund-raising events like a quiz or barbecue, to help with purchases like music, instruments, and workbooks.

Is it a big commitment? 


Our normal routine is:
– Choir practice: Wednesdays in term-time from 6.30 – 7.45pm
– One Sunday morning service per month (including rehearsal): 9.15 – 11.15 (usually on the first Sunday of each month in term-time)

Occasionally there are optional extra services, and events like weddings (for which choristers are paid!) Our “senior-juniors” (those in year 6 and above) also get additional opportunities to sing with the senior choir, if they wish to do so. We don’t sing in the school holidays, nor are there Wednesday practices for juniors in half-terms (though there usually is a Half-term Singing Event in the autumn and spring).

Who can join?


Children may join the Junior Choir from school Year 3, and are usually aged 7 to 15. Prior experience and musical skills are not necessary, and children do not need a religious background. We only look for enthusiasm and a willingness to sing! After year 9 (and sometimes before), young choristers normally rehearse and sing with the adult members of the choir (Wednesdays 7.30-8.30pm) with continued training available for those following the “Voice for Life” programme.

The choir also offers opportunities to sing at other events, cathedrals, and singing courses such as the RSCM singing course, which was held in Beverley, Yorkshire, in 2025 (see photos below).

What's the next step?


If you’d like to discuss the choir further, come and see us at St Michael’s on a Sunday or email us. We’re always very happy to have an informal chat with parents.

Children need parental consent to participate in the choir. You can fill in your details and request your starting date by clicking on the button below…

13. About Opportunity:
Brave Island is the creative network for young people aged 14-25 on the Isle of Wight, with over 600 members and growing!

Created by Ventnor Exchange, we post new opportunities including mentoring, funding, trips, workshops, experiences and training for young people every week.

Mentoring sessions are part of our affordable music tuition offer, but we also work with education settings, charities and organisations who support young people to offer free sessions. 
If you work with young people who may face barriers to accessing music, get in touch to request a Priority Access Code.

About the sessions:
Music Mentoring Sessions are run by Southampton and Isle of Wight Music, a partnership of organisations, which are led by Southampton City Council. Sessions are available in both Southampton and Isle of Wight, at:
Southampton — Mayflower Studios, City Centre
IOW — The Department, Ryde
These sessions are for young people aged 11–18 who want help to create and release music. We welcome singers, rappers, songwriters, producers and other musicians!
With music mentoring, you will get to work with a music expert to craft beats, write lyrics and produce tracks, as well as getting support to release and promote your own music!
It doesn’t matter if you’re just starting out, not sure where to go next or have big ambitions - our team are here to help!

A teenage musical theatre group based in Oxford, producing one show per year, alternating between the Oxford Playhouse and another Oxford Theatre.

Singers, dancers and actors from the Oxfordshire Youth Music Theatre are a group of talented 12 to 18-year-olds from across the county.

Over the last few years OYMT has built a reputation of presenting high quality youth theatre, with many former members now enjoying successful professional careers in West End hits such as Rock of Ages and Wicked, and even the 25th and 30th anniversary performances of Les Misérables.

The Fidelio Charitable Trust was established in 2008, thanks to an endowment from the late Tony Wingate, in support of the Arts in the United Kingdom. Tony was an inspirational teacher and had a great love of the arts, particularly music.

The Trustees aim to help individuals and groups who would not be able to carry out a project or activity without financial support, particularly those who are at an early stage in their careers. Such individuals and groups must be nominated by an appropriate institution, college, Arts Festival or similar organisation in the UK, preferably a registered charity. Applications will not be considered from those seeking support for themselves or their school age children (e.g. for course or competition fees).

The person representing the nominating institution or organisation must vouch for the competence and skill of those for whom they are applying and state how they were selected. They should explain their professional relationship with the individual or group and describe accurately the project or activity for which money is required and by when. Funding is not available for work already undertaken.

It is important to give as much detail as possible about how much money is needed and how the proposed budget is made up. Fidelio's funds are limited and no single grant will normally be greater than £5,000. The trustees, while willing to provide support, may sometimes only be able to part-fund a project or activity.

ideas, meet new friends, visit new places and explore music like never before.

13. About Opportunity:
If you are a local musician aged 12-18 (KS3 or above) and play any instrument at Grade 3 level or above, come and join the NYO Open event, a free creative day of music-making, during NYO Unite in London.

Connect with other young musicians, learn from our creative team and NYO alumni, and play your part in creating a brand-new piece, taking inspiration from orchestral music that NYO and NYO Inspire musicians will be exploring throughout the day. You’ll be amazed how easy it is to make music with new people.

At the end of the day, you’ll showcase your creation in a special NYO Unite sharing performance for your family and friends. Plus, you’ll get to enjoy a live performance by other musicians participating in NYO Unite, featuring the very music that inspired your own piece.

Join our super friendly community to explore music, get creative and have fun playing together.

13. About Opportunity:
We help young people from the least privileged backgrounds in society, where the wealth and income resources available within their family are limited and the musical opportunities available to them are restricted.  We particularly welcome applications from individuals within single parent families.

Our funding awards are targeted at individuals who will be aged 14 to 18 at the time the activity will take place, although in exceptional cases we can offer support to individuals who will be aged 13 or 19.

Our awards are not targeted at beneficiaries with proven outstanding musical talent or ability.  We welcome applications on behalf of those who have already demonstrated great promise of course, but just as much from those who may have only comparatively recently begun the journey of personal musical discovery.  The key question for us is:  “How much difference would our award make to generating for this individual a lifelong love of making music and allowing them to realize their dreams?”

"I went on from the Four Counties Youth Orchestra to play in the National Youth Orchestra of Wales and in two weeks' time I will be joining the Royal Welsh College of Music and Drama! Thank you once again for this hugely important funding without which I would not have been able to progress as I now have"
- Nicholas, Cardiff (Violin, FCYO residential course, 2015)

Awards are restricted to those who play instruments and we are unable to assist those who wish to develop their singing or vocal skills.  We expect a beneficiary to have reached at least ABRSM Grade 4 or equivalent standard in their chosen instrument, and the majority of awards so far have gone to individuals at or close to Grade 8 standard.  We need to be confident that they have a commitment to learning, and that they will benefit substantially from the activity identified in the application.

Awards are provided for attendance at specific events.  Although that may in appropriate cases be extended to two or more linked events, we do not assist with the costs of having regular lessons or tuition.  Nor do we assist with costs associated with being awarded a place at a private educational institution such as boarding school, sixth form or college.

We aim to benefit those of secondary school age (Year 7) to 18 years. Reviews are conducted twice a year, with deadlines at 1st February and 1st September.
Disbursements include payment for lessons, choir/orchestra tour fees, music colleges etc.
Assistance varies according to circumstances. Distributions have ranged between £100 and £800.
Grants are either "one offs" or reviewable annual payments for a specific period.
Three grants per person is normally the maximum the trust gives.
Two letters of support from the candidate's tutors/teachers.
Various Grants open throughout the year

13. About Opportunity:
Make new friends, pursue your creative potential and explore exciting music!

The West of England Youth Orchestra (WEYO) is a symphony orchestra that provides young musicians aged 12-21 from Wiltshire and the surrounding area with opportunities to perform with other instrumentalists and learn from leading professionals.

Principally conducted by Tim Redmond (professor of conducting at Guildhall School, London), WEYO meets at WMC three times per year during school holidays for non-residential courses lasting between 4 and 7 days, culminating in concerts in our world-class auditorium. Working with your conductor, a team of exceptional instrumental tutors and totally inspiring guest artists, you will explore new repertoire and push your musical development to new heights!

For each course, WEYO is joined by an inspirational professional soloist – recent guests have included Charlotte Saluste-Bridoux (violin), Ariel Lanyi (piano), Alice Neary (cello), Ben Goldscheider (French horn), Alexandra Dariescu (piano) and Emily Apps (voice).

Up next for WEYO… Wallace & Gromit!

We’re excited to announce that WEYO’s next project in April 2026 will be Wallace & Gromit’s ‘The Wrong Trousers’! In this fun and entertaining family-friendly show, the orchestra will be playing along live to a screening of this iconic animation film, as well as performing other treats designed to introduce the orchestra to an audience of all ages!

13. About Opportunity:
Come along to Wiltshire Jazz Academy! We welcome musicians aged 12-18 who are playing confidently (approximately Grade 4 or equivalent). You do not need to have experience in performing jazz music to join and the sessions are open to all instrumentalists.

The ensemble was created in partnership with NYJO as a Regional Academy to provide more opportunity in the South West for young people to access high quality jazz tuition. Led by jazz tutor Mike Daniels and visiting guest artists, it provides a perfect progression route to joining Wiltshire Youth Jazz Orchestra, and is a valuable opportunity for jazz musicians at all stages of their development.
